title: India-UK FTA and Double Contributions Convention (DCC)
focus: Provisions of the India-UK Free Trade Agreement and the specifics of the reciprocal Double Contributions Convention.
rationale: This section covers a specific bilateral agreement, highlighting unique features like the DCC and procurement access, which are prime targets for statement-based questions.
sectionExcerpt: Details the India-UK Free Trade Agreement, highlighting zero-duty market access for approximately 99% of Indian exports, mobility for professionals, and UK participation in Indian government procurement. It also covers the reciprocal Double Contributions Convention (DCC), a Social Security Agreement ensuring employees moving between countries pay social security in only one country for up to 3 years.
evidenceBullets: DCC is a Social Security Agreement ensuring employees temporarily working in the other country (up to 3 years) pay contributions in only one country at a time.
India-UK FTA provides zero-duty market access for approximately 99% of Indian exports.
India will cut levies on 90% of British products sold in the country.
The FTA allows UK businesses to compete for central government and state-owned enterprise procurements in India.

title: Basics of Free Trade Agreements and Trade Hierarchy
focus: Conceptual understanding of FTAs, Early Harvest Schemes, and the hierarchy of economic integration.
rationale: Core static economy concepts regarding trade agreements and their hierarchy are crucial for UPSC Prelims conceptual clarity.
sectionExcerpt: Explains Free Trade Agreements (FTAs) as arrangements reducing tariff and non-tariff barriers, acting as an exception to the WTO's Most Favoured Nation (MFN) principle. It outlines the hierarchy of trade agreements from Preferential Trade Agreements (PTA) and FTAs to deeper integrations like Custom Unions, Common Markets, and Economic Unions.
evidenceBullets: FTAs act as an exception to the Most Favoured Nation (MFN) principle of the World Trade Organisation (WTO).
Early Harvest Scheme is a precursor to an FTA and other trade agreements.
Custom Union involves zero duty trade among member countries and common tariffs against the rest of the world.
Common Market allows free movement of labour and capital, along with harmonized technical standards.
Economic Union is the deepest level of integration, featuring common economic policies and institutions.

title: India-EFTA Trade and Economic Partnership Agreement (TEPA)
focus: Key features, member nations, and binding commitments of the India-EFTA TEPA.
rationale: A major recent trade agreement with unique binding investment commitments, making it highly relevant for current affairs questions.
sectionExcerpt: Covers the India-EFTA TEPA, involving Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland. It is India's first FTA with a binding commitment linked to investment ($100 billion) and job creation (1 million direct jobs in 15 years). The pact includes Mutual Recognition Agreements (MRAs) in professional services and substantial market access for goods.
evidenceBullets: EFTA nations include Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland, with Switzerland being India's largest trading partner among them.
TEPA is the first Indian FTA to incorporate a binding commitment of $100 billion investment and 1 million direct jobs over the next 15 years.
The agreement enables Mutual Recognition Agreements (MRAs) in professional services such as nursing, chartered accountancy, and architecture.
EFTA offers substantial market access, covering 100% of non-agricultural products.

title: Export Promotion Mission (EPM) and India's Export Landscape
focus: Provisions, architecture, and objectives of the Export Promotion Mission, along with macroeconomic data on India's exports.
rationale: A newly approved government mission with specific financial outlays, sub-schemes, and critical macroeconomic data points regarding India's export sector.
sectionExcerpt: Details the Union Cabinet's approval of the Export Promotion Mission (EPM) and the Credit Guarantee Scheme for Exporters (CGSE). EPM has a financial outlay of Rs. 25,060 crores over six years, implemented by the DGFT. It operates via Niryat Protsahan (financial support) and Niryat Disha (non-financial support). India's exports contribute 21% to GDP, with MSMEs accounting for 45% of total exports.
evidenceBullets: EPM has a financial outlay of Rs. 25,060 crores for a six-year timeline (FY 2025-26 to FY 2030-31).
The mission is implemented by the Directorate General of Foreign Trade (DGFT).
Niryat Protsahan provides financial support for affordable trade finance, while Niryat Disha provides non-financial support to enhance market readiness.
India's export sector contributes 21% to overall GDP (FY 2024-25).
MSMEs contribute 45% of India's total exports.
India's share in global exports of goods and services is currently at around 2.5%.
